title: Sealed Classes (Java 17)
objectives: Understand sealed types via a real-life analogy
Combine sealed + records for algebraic data types

sections.markdown:
**Think of a menu at a small restaurant.**

- A **regular class hierarchy** is like a food court — anyone can open a new stall selling anything, and the food court has no way to list *all* possible foods. That's what a normal `class Shape` gives you: any code, anywhere, can `extends Shape` and add a new subtype. You can never write a truly complete list of shapes.
- A **sealed class** is like a curated tasting menu: 'This restaurant serves EXACTLY these three dishes — Pizza, Pasta, or Salad. That's the whole menu.' The menu is *closed*. If a customer asks 'do you have burgers?', the waiter can say NO with certainty because burgers aren't in the permits list.

**Why this matters:** once the compiler knows the complete list of possibilities, it can PROVE that your `switch` handled every case. If tomorrow you add 'Dessert' to the menu, every switch that doesn't handle Dessert stops compiling — no more 'forgot a case' bugs.

**A sealed type explicitly lists which classes may extend it.** `sealed class Shape permits Circle, Square, Triangle {}` — only Circle, Square, and Triangle may `extends Shape`, and each of them must be `final`, `sealed`, or `non-sealed`. This closes the hierarchy so the compiler can reason exhaustively about it.
## Syntax — the three permitted-subtype variants
## Rules the compiler enforces

rows: Every permitted subtype must be `final`, `sealed`, or `non-sealed`
No middle ground — you must decide the sealing at each level
All permitted subtypes must be in the same module (or same package if unnamed module)
The `permits` list can be omitted if all subtypes are in the same file
For small hierarchies, you can skip it
Sealed + records + pattern-matching switch → exhaustive matching
The compiler proves every case is handled — no default needed

sections.markdown: **The key insight:** sealed classes solve the OOP paradox — inheritance is powerful but also uncontrolled. Before Java 17, if you wrote `class Shape`, anyone could add a `class Hexagon extends Shape`, and your `switch` on shapes would silently ignore it. Sealed types put you in charge of the hierarchy again, letting the compiler catch missed cases.
## A complete sample program — sealed + records + exhaustive switch
## Common beginner mistakes

**Mistake 1: Forgetting to mark permitted subtypes as `final` / `sealed` / `non-sealed`.**

`sealed interface Shape permits Circle {}` — but if `Circle` is a plain class (not `final` or `sealed` or `non-sealed`), the code won't compile. Every permitted subtype must explicitly declare its own sealing.

**Mistake 2: Cross-module permits list.**

Sealed hierarchies must fit within a single module (or single package for unnamed modules). You cannot have a `permits` list that names classes in another module. This is intentional — sealing is a strong coupling.

**Mistake 3: Confusing sealed with `final`.**

`final` means 'no subtypes at all'. `sealed` means 'only THESE specific subtypes'. Sealed is the middle ground between wide-open inheritance and no inheritance.

**Mistake 4: Adding `default:` to an exhaustive switch.**

Once your switch handles every permitted subtype, a `default:` is unreachable — but worse, adding a `default` disables the exhaustiveness check. Now if you add a new subtype, the switch silently ignores it. Skip the `default` on sealed-type switches.
## Best practices
**1. Use sealed + record + switch for algebraic data types.** This is the modern Java pattern for closed domain models — Result, Option, tree nodes, expression evaluators, state machines.
**2. Prefer `final` for leaves.** Only use `sealed` at the leaf level if you genuinely need another level of hierarchy.
**3. Skip the `default:` on exhaustive switches.** The whole point is that the compiler catches missing cases; a `default` hides that.
**4. Keep the hierarchy small.** If your permits list has 20 items, it's a sign that class-per-behavior might not be the best shape — consider strategy or enum.
**5. Combine with records.** Records + sealed = zero-boilerplate ADTs. This is one of Java's biggest steps toward Kotlin/Scala/Rust ergonomics.
## Practice programs

**Practice 1 (Easy) — Model a payment status.** `sealed interface PaymentStatus permits Pending, Paid, Refunded, Failed`. Write an exhaustive switch that returns a display message.

**Why:** Sealed types shine for state machines.

**Practice 2 (Medium) — Result<T> type.** `sealed interface Result<T> permits Success<T>, Failure`. Add `map` and `orElse` methods.

**Why:** This replaces null/exception patterns with a compiler-checked one.

**Practice 3 (Hard) — HTTP response modeling.** Model `sealed interface HttpResponse permits Ok, Redirect, ClientError, ServerError` (with a status code in each) and switch to handle each family.

**Why:** Real-world APIs are naturally sealed hierarchies.

**Practice 4 (Expert) — Expression tree.** `sealed interface Expr permits Num, Add, Mul, Neg` and evaluate + print. Add `Sub` and watch every switch fail to compile until you handle it.

**Why:** Classic AST modeling with compile-time exhaustiveness — this is what makes writing a mini interpreter fun in modern Java.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What is a sealed class and why was it introduced?**

**A:** A sealed class (Java 17) is a class or interface that explicitly lists which classes may extend/implement it via `permits`. Before sealed types, Java's inheritance was open — anyone could subclass your public class from anywhere in the codebase. This made it impossible to reason about the complete set of subtypes, so switches over type hierarchies could silently miss new subtypes. Sealed types close the hierarchy, giving the compiler enough information to enforce exhaustive pattern matching. This eliminates 'forgot a case' bugs and enables ergonomic algebraic data types (sealed + records + pattern switch).

**Q2: What are the three modifiers a permitted subtype can have?**

**A:** Every permitted subtype must be one of: **(1) `final`** — cannot be subclassed further; the leaf of the hierarchy. Most common choice. **(2) `sealed`** — introduces another level of controlled hierarchy; must have its own `permits` list. **(3) `non-sealed`** — explicitly opts back out; the subtype can be extended freely by anyone. Useful when part of the hierarchy is open-ended (e.g. `non-sealed class UserException extends AppException` — a controlled family plus a wildcard for extension). The compiler forces you to pick one; there is no default.

**Q4: When would you use `non-sealed`?**

**A:** When part of a sealed hierarchy needs to remain open for extension by unknown code. Example: `sealed interface Exception permits IOException, AppException, non-sealed UserException`. Here you want `IOException` and `AppException` to be closed families, but `UserException` is meant to be subclassed by application code you haven't seen. `non-sealed` says 'this branch of the tree opts back out of the sealing constraint.' It's the escape hatch. Use it sparingly — if half your permits list is `non-sealed`, you've lost most of the benefit of sealing.

**Q6: Sealed classes vs enums — when to use which?**

**A:** Use an **enum** when each case is a *singleton value* with a fixed identity (`RED`, `GREEN`, `BLUE`; `PENDING`, `PAID`, `FAILED`). Use a **sealed hierarchy** when each case is a *type* that carries its own data (`Circle(radius)`, `Square(side)`; `Success<T>(value)`, `Failure(error)`). Enums are simpler and more concise for pure-value cases; sealed types are more expressive when the cases carry different shapes of data. Both give exhaustive switching. A good rule: if you find yourself adding fields via `enum X { A(1), B(2); ...}` for each constant, and the fields differ, promote to a sealed hierarchy.

**Q7: Where must permitted subtypes live relative to the sealed parent?**

**A:** They must be in the **same module** (for named modules) or the **same package** (for unnamed modules — i.e. classpath-based apps). Also, if all subtypes are in the *same file* as the sealed parent, you can omit the `permits` clause entirely. This module/package restriction is intentional — sealing is a strong contract between the parent and its subtypes, and letting them be defined arbitrarily far away would undermine the guarantee.

## Real-world usage

- **State machines** — order status, connection state, user session lifecycle.
- **Result/Either types** — Success | Failure, with `map`/`flatMap` — a compiler-checked alternative to exceptions.
- **HTTP responses** — Ok, Redirect, ClientError, ServerError — each carrying different data.
- **Command/event modeling** — commands and events in CQRS are naturally sealed.
- **AST / expression trees** — parsers, compilers, DSLs.
- **Domain events in event sourcing** — a bounded set of what can happen.

## Advanced notes

- **Sealed permitting sealed** — hierarchies can be arbitrarily deep, each level closed independently.
- **Reflection** — `Class.isSealed()`, `Class.getPermittedSubclasses()` — used by frameworks that need to enumerate the hierarchy.
- **Compile-time exhaustiveness** — Java 21+ makes this fully seamless; earlier versions needed `--enable-preview`.
- **Interaction with modules** — `permits` classes must be in the same module; useful pattern is `module-info.java` `exports com.app.model` where the sealed types live.
- **Serialization** — sealed hierarchies serialize fine (Jackson handles the polymorphism via `@JsonTypeInfo` or Jackson 2.15+ native sealed-type support).
- **Migration** — you can retrofit `sealed` onto existing classes if you own all the subtypes; if you don't, you'll need to declare `non-sealed` at each unknown extension point.

flashcards.back: The type explicitly lists (via `permits`) which classes may extend it.
Closes the hierarchy so the compiler can enforce exhaustive pattern matching.
Algebraic data types with zero boilerplate + compile-time exhaustiveness.
Enum = fixed singletons; sealed = fixed types each carrying different data.

options: Final = no subtypes; sealed = only listed subtypes
quiz.explain: Sealed is the middle ground between no-inheritance and open-inheritance.
The compiler forces the choice at every level.

quiz.q: Best combo for algebraic data types?

options: This subtype opts BACK OUT and can be extended freely
quiz.explain: Escape hatch for open-ended parts of a sealed hierarchy.

quiz.q: Sealed permits list must be in…
options: Same module (or same package for unnamed modules)

challenges.text: Write `sealed interface PaymentStatus permits Pending, Paid, Refunded, Failed` and exhaustively switch to a display string.
Explain the difference between `final`, `sealed`, and `non-sealed` with a code example of each.
Write `sealed interface Result<T> permits Success<T>, Failure` and add `map` + `orElse`.
Model `sealed interface HttpResponse permits Ok, Redirect, ClientError, ServerError` (with a status code).
Retrofit sealed types onto an existing hierarchy — add `permits` and choose `final`/`sealed`/`non-sealed` at each subtype.
Add a `Pentagon` subtype to a sealed `Shape` hierarchy and watch every exhaustive switch break — then fix them.
Model a small AST (Num, Add, Mul, Neg) with sealed + records and evaluate it with pattern matching.

Sealed classes vs enums — when to use which?
Where must permitted subtypes live relative to the sealed parent?
